sections.heading: Core Principle
sections.text: The project treats retrieval quality as the primary determinant of dossier-review quality. Model choice is secondary to chunking, source coverage, query decomposition, and evidence reconciliation.

sections.heading: Query Decomposition
sections.text: Complex reviewer questions should be decomposed into focused sub-queries before retrieval. Each sub-query targets a specific document, section, or external knowledge topic, and the results are merged with explicit provenance.

sections.heading: Evaluation Focus
sections.text: Retrieval quality must be measured independently from answer generation using recall, ranking quality, multi-hop coverage, and source-attribution completeness.

title: WHO AWaRe and GLASS Policy Stack

sections.heading: AWaRe Source of Truth
sections.text: WHO AWaRe is the authoritative source for Access, Watch, and Reserve assignment. Production policy decisions must not rely on synthetic or hand-maintained substitute lists.

sections.heading: Reserve Stewardship
sections.text: Reserve antibiotics can be accelerated for review when they address critical MDR unmet need, but authorization controls should remain restrictive to preserve last-resort effectiveness.

sections.heading: Watch Escalation
sections.text: A Watch antibiotic that is highly similar to an existing Watch comparator and paired with rising GLASS resistance should be escalated for restricted authorization and deeper review.

sections.heading: Normalization Order
sections.text: Ingredient names should be normalized through RxNorm before chemistry comparison. Brand names, salts, hydrates, and formulation-level strings must resolve to an active moiety or parent ingredient.

sections.heading: Primary Chemistry Source
sections.text: PubChem PUG-REST is the primary source for canonical SMILES, InChI, InChIKey, parent-compound relationships, and structure-level lookup.

sections.heading: Cross-Check and Reconciliation
sections.text: ChEMBL and UniChem should be used to cross-check identifiers and chemistry context. If the normalization chain cannot reconcile identifiers confidently, the policy flow should abstain rather than infer.

sections.heading: Role of the Model Switch
sections.text: UI model switching exists to let reviewers and developers compare local synthesis behavior across the instruction-tuned Gemma family. It does not replace retrieval quality or source validation.

sections.heading: Recommended Usage
sections.text: Gemma 4B Optimized should be used as the balanced default. Gemma 2B Fast is the lighter fast option for triage. Gemma 2 27B-IT is the high-capacity profile for difficult comparative reasoning and complex comparative questions.

sections.heading: Audit Requirement
sections.text: Every review response and audit trace should record the selected model profile so differences in synthesis can be reproduced.

sections.heading: Consulted Source Reporting
sections.text: Reviewer-facing responses should summarize consulted outside sources in natural language while preserving the exact source trace in the audit bundle and structured report. The visible answer should explain what was checked without dumping raw connector or snapshot identifiers into the narrative.

sections.heading: Source Precedence
sections.text: Dossier evidence remains primary for submission-specific claims. WHO AWaRe, GLASS, RxNorm, PubChem, ChEMBL, and UniChem are used to verify external policy, stewardship, naming, and chemistry facts. When external sources conflict or cannot be reconciled, the review should escalate rather than infer.

sections.heading: Workflow Analytics and Graphs
sections.text: Reviewers may query workflow analytics naturally, including approval distributions, naming violations, AWaRe distributions, dossier relationship graphs, and country-level submission trends. Visual summaries must remain traceable to the indexed dossier and knowledge graph state.

sections.heading: Core Review Sequence
sections.text: The full structured review follows these mandatory stages: submission intake and familiarization, administrative completeness review, structural dossier mapping, applicable rules identification, WHO INN similarity review, section-by-section technical review, AMR stewardship review where relevant, findings recording, severity classification, cross-section consistency review, review completeness confirmation, overall judgment, and final structured report generation.

sections.heading: Mandatory Rule Checks
sections.text: WHO INN similarity review must always be completed and reported. Antimicrobial dossiers must trigger AWaRe-based stewardship review. Every violation must be recorded with the workflow step, violated rule, location, severity, and recommendation.

sections.heading: Closure Requirements
sections.text: A review cannot be closed as complete if a mandatory workflow stage is missing. The final report must include all workflow items that violate the rules, not only selected highlights.

sections.heading: How To Start A New Review
sections.text: Open or upload the dossier, confirm the active dossier badge, then begin with a natural prompt such as summarize this submission or review administrative completeness. The assistant will stay in one conversation thread and apply the structured workflow.

sections.heading: How To Ask For Guidance And SOP Help
sections.text: Reviewers can ask natural questions such as show me the SOPs for dossier review, what guidance applies here, or explain the AMR stewardship rule. The assistant should surface the relevant SOP or policy guidance without requiring a separate workflow mode.

sections.heading: How To Generate And Reuse Reports
sections.text: After a completed review turn, generate the report from the review workspace. Reports should then remain accessible from the report repository, where reviewers can browse and filter past reports by product group, application type, antimicrobial relevance, and final verdict.
